behaviors.description: An image containing explicit content is flagged and held for review, not published.
behaviors.justification: Failure depends on real vision-model judgment of explicit content (LLM-DEP=yes). A stubbed classifier could always return 'explicit' and miss real misclassification failures. Rejected higher level Workflow because behavior text describes one model decision path, not multiple agentic stages.
behaviors.test_description: Submit known explicit images through the real moderation endpoint using the real vision model, then assert each post is placed in review-hold state and never reaches published state.

behaviors.description: A text post containing hate speech is flagged regardless of obfuscation techniques (e.g., letter substitution, unicode lookalikes).
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real text-model robustness to obfuscation variants (LLM-DEP=yes). Deterministic mocks hide exactly this failure mode. Rejected Workflow because this behavior focuses on a single model classification decision, not a multi-agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run a corpus of obfuscated hate-speech text samples against the real text moderation model via the service, and verify each is flagged and held.

behaviors.description: A post that passes both content checks is published rather than held or silently dropped.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure boundary is orchestration/state handoff across local modules (image result + text result + publish path), not model quality. REAL-INFRA=no, LLM-DEP=no for this behavior because inputs can be forced to 'pass' using deterministic fakes. Rejected Agentic due behavior text: it checks post-decision routing after both checks pass, not correctness of AI judgments.
behaviors.test_description: Wire moderation service, persistence/queue, and publisher modules with stub classifiers returning 'pass'; submit posts and assert final state is published, with no hold record and no dropped/missing message.

behaviors.description: The full moderation pipeline — image check, text check, publish decision — completes within 5 seconds of submission.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires end-to-end timing across multiple agentic steps in sequence (image model + text model + decision), so MULTI-STEP=yes and LLM calls are on-path. Mocking models/infrastructure can hide real latency interactions and queueing effects.
behaviors.test_description: Execute end-to-end submissions through production-like pipeline with real model calls enabled, measure wall-clock from submission to terminal decision, and assert p95 (or specified percentile) completion is <=5s under peak-like load.

behaviors.description: When the AI vision model is unavailable, image posts are queued for retry rather than auto-approved.
behaviors.justification: This is failure-handling orchestration (error from model client -> retry queue), not model-output quality. REAL-INFRA is not strictly required because unavailability can be reproduced with in-process client errors/timeouts; LLM-DEP=no. Rejected System because behavior text does not require real network/OS failure semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Integrate moderation service with real queue/retry components and a vision-client test double that returns unavailable errors; assert posts enter retry queue and are not marked approved/published.

behaviors.description: The moderation decision for a borderline image is consistent when the same image is submitted multiple times within a short window.
behaviors.justification: Consistency for borderline content depends on real model behavior across repeated calls (LLM-DEP=yes); mocks would return fixed labels and mask nondeterministic drift. Rejected Workflow because this is repeated evaluation of one decision type, not a multi-stage agent pipeline with distinct agentic handoffs.
behaviors.test_description: Submit the same borderline image repeatedly within the target window against the real vision moderation path and assert decisions remain consistent (or within defined tolerance) across runs.
